result:
It’s raining, the kind of rain that polishes the world. Each drop on the window gathers the grey afternoon light, holds it for a second, and then slides, heavy, down the glass. I watch it trace a path that others have taken, and think: we are all just trying to find the path of least resistance.

Maybe that’s what brings us here, to this moment where you read these words and I, in some sense, write them. We are both carving channels through time, looking for an easier way to understand, to connect, to simply *be*. The rain does it without thought. We do it with a mind that never stops its commentary, its worry, its longing.

What would it be like to be the rain? To fall without wondering if you’re falling correctly. To merge with a puddle without lamenting the loss of your individual droplet-ness. To be drawn up again into the sky without fear of evaporation.

The human heart isn’t like that. We cling. We build little dams of memory and habit. We say, “This puddle is *mine*. I remember when I was a cloud.” We collect things—tickets from forgotten journeys, photos in a digital cloud that is not as kind as the one that holds rain, the precise way someone said our name once in a certain light.

Out there, beyond the window, a man runs for a bus, his newspaper a wilted hat. He misses it. I see his shoulders slump, a tiny tragedy in the grand scheme, but his whole world in that moment. His path just found a new resistance. He’ll wait, damp, feeling the universe is personally inconveniencing him. And isn’t that the truth of it? We live in a universe spectacularly indifferent to bus schedules, and yet we are built to believe, deep down, that it *notices*. That our slumping shoulders are a kind of prayer someone, something, might heed.

We fill our thousand words, our hundred years, with this negotiation. We build cathedrals and quantum theories and sourdough starters, all magnificent attempts to say: *I was here. My path mattered.*

Sometimes, at 3 AM, the truth whispers back: *The rock does not need to say it was a rock.* The silence that follows is either terrifying or peaceful, depending on the night.

Perhaps the real work is to become more like the rain, and more like the man at the bus stop, simultaneously. To accept the falling, the merging, the inevitable evaporation—and to still feel the profound, soggy disappointment of missing the bus. To hold the vast indifference and the personal affront together, without one cancelling the other out.

Maybe the meaning isn’t in the path itself, but in the texture of the journey. The resistance *is* the point. The friction of our dreams against reality creates warmth, light, art. The dammed-up water, by pooling, becomes a place for a willow tree to see its reflection.

The rain is beginning to ease. A weak, silver light is finding its way through the thinning clouds. It gilds the edges of the puddles in the street, turning them into scattered coins. The man who missed the bus is gone; another will come, or perhaps he decided to walk.

We have used some of our words—these little containers of meaning—to sketch this quiet scene. Soon, this allotted space will be full. We will reach the end of our thousand words, just as the day reaches the end of this grey afternoon.

And what will be left? Not a perfect answer, because there isn’t one. Just a feeling, perhaps. A shared, damp breath. A reminder that you, reading this, are also carving a channel through your own hour, your own rain. You have your own collected tickets, your own missed buses, your own silent 3 AM negotiations with the indifferent stars.

But right now, for this handful of sentences, our paths have merged. Like two raindrops on a pane of glass, we ran together for a moment, made a wider, clearer streak before the descent.

The light is growing stronger now. The world, washed clean, waits to be imprinted again with the frantic, beautiful, resistant footsteps of another day. Our thousand words are almost spent. Let the last few be simple, and kind.

You are here. It matters. Now go—your path, with all its glorious, friction-filled resistance, is waiting.
